oai_dc:dc.dc:title: A cross-sectional survey exploring veterinarian perspectives on end-of-life decision-making for beef and dairy cattle
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Edwards-Callaway, Lily
McBride, Brianna
Keller, Kayleigh P.
Middlemist, Emily
Cramer, Catie
Roman-Muniz, Noa
Varnum, Angela
Alvarez, Diego
Stallones, Lorann
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: cull
euthanasia
fitness-for-transport
slaughter
welfare
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: End-of-life decisions for cull cattle directly impact animal welfare, farm economics, and food safety, yet these choices are often delayed or inconsistent. To better understand vet­erinarian perspectives on this issue, a national survey was developed grounded in the Social Ecological Model (SEM) framework. The survey was distributed to veterinarians in the United States working primarily with beef or dairy cattle (n = 74). Respondents indicated that they had adequate re­sources to decide and provide timely euthanasia for cattle (Strongly Agree n = 64, 86.5%, Agree n = 8, 10.8%) and to identify a cow unfit for transport (Strongly Agree n = 33, 44.6%, Agree n = 39, 52.7%). Almost half of veterinarians par­ticipated in euthanasia decision-making with their clients (Strongly Agree n = 4, 5.4%, Agree n = 32, 43.2%) and reported high agreement with their clients regarding these decisions (Strongly Agree n = 3, 4.1%, Agree n = 62, 83.8%). Fewer vet­erinarians were involved in decisions to ship cows to slaugh­ter (Strongly Agree n = 1, 1.4%, Agree n = 21, 28.4%), with less agreement with clients on these decisions than about eutha­nasia (Strongly Agree n = 2, 2.7%, Agree n = 54, 73.0%). A dis­connect existed in veterinarians’ reported knowledge of and contact with the rest of the supply chain, with inconsistent feedback shared from their clients about cows who arrive in poor condition at the plant. There is an opportunity for veteri­narians to ensure their clients have protocols and training in place for euthanasia and fitness-for-transport decisions.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: The Bovine Practitioner; Vol. 60 No. 2 (2026): Online First; 1-17
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
